title: Coming down into Brae Hollow, the young hollow loud with living
Sera at the Cistern Steps: the upper spring and catch-basin are hers to mind
Sera finds a creeping rot in the basin's lower vent
Sera packs and seals the rot off course by course
Sera cuts her keeper's mark beside the sealed vent
The cistern runs sweet; the rot is sealed away below
Halden the boundary-mason in the busy Loom Quarter
A dispute over which river crossing the dye-carts may use
Halden reads the river and finds the one firm ford
Halden cuts a boundary-stone to mark the firm ford
Halden beds the stone at the head of the firm ford
For Halden it is only a cart-dispute settled; drought never crosses his mind
The Wend Fields, green and worked, in a good year
The bearer enters the young hollow with the wend-pipe
The bearer watches Sera seal the vent, files it as ordinary
The bearer watches Halden bed the boundary-stone at the ford
The wend-pipe moves the bearer between the two ages on one spot
Back at the Wend crossing with the new understanding
A dry gorge cuts the way to the idle far field
A field-elder gives the bearer a slow gorge-sapling
The bearer plays into the withered age: spring down to a thread
The young hollow is the old hollow's cause; one place before and after
Back at the Wend crossing, the sapling still in hand
Weighing whether the far field is worth the planting
At dusk, kneeling at the lip of the gorge with the sapling
The fork: plant the gorge-sapling at the lip, or do not
Three generations on, Brae Hollow under a long drought
Marn keeps the cistern, the last good water in the valley
Marn does not know why his cistern alone runs clean
The bearer recognises the same vent at the Cistern Steps
The cistern runs clean; the bearer finds the keeper's mark in the stone
The Loom Quarter gone silent; Pell the last weaver
Pell knows nothing of the old cart-dispute or who settled it
Ode the field-warden keeps the dying crossing
Ode digs up the old boundary-stone marking the firm ford
The Withering folk cross on the firm ford the old stone marks
The bridge-tree spans the gorge, grown from the gorge-sapling
Tamsin works the far field, crossing the bridge-tree daily
The bearer crosses back and sees the causes: the seal and the stone
Brae Hollow withers but is not lost; the things that hold, hold
